issues.claim: Unit 1 shows up on roughly 8 to 11 percent of the AP exam.
issues.problem: Matches the current AP Biology CED weighting for Unit 1 (8-11%). Accurate; flagged only because exam weights occasionally shift between CED revisions and should be re-verified yearly.
issues.suggested_fix: No change needed; verify against the latest College Board CED each summer.

issues.claim: Each hydrogen bond is about five percent of a covalent bond.
issues.problem: Reasonable order-of-magnitude figure (H-bond ~20 kJ/mol vs O-H covalent ~460 kJ/mol = ~4-5%), but exact ratio depends on the covalent reference. AP graders accept the qualitative point; the specific 5% number is defensible but cherry-picked.
issues.suggested_fix: Optional softening: 'roughly one-twentieth the strength of a typical covalent bond' avoids implying a single canonical ratio.

issues.claim: Methyl is nonpolar and marks DNA and proteins.
issues.problem: Correct (methylation is a real epigenetic and post-translational mark), but slightly beyond Unit 1 scope. Students may infer methylation is a Unit 1 topic when it actually surfaces in Unit 6 (Gene Expression). Not an error, just a scope note.
issues.suggested_fix: Optionally add: 'You'll see methylation again in Unit 6 when we cover gene regulation.'

issues.claim: Per OpenStax Chapter Three-Four, proteins serve as enzymes, structural support like collagen and keratin, transporters like hemoglobin, and defenders like antibodies.
issues.problem: The mid-narration citation 'per OpenStax Chapter Three-Four' is awkward when spoken aloud and reads as a pipeline artifact rather than natural teaching language. Factually the content (protein roles) is correct and OpenStax 3.4 is indeed the Proteins section, so no factual error — only stylistic / rigor concern that it sounds machine-generated.
issues.suggested_fix: Drop the citation phrase: 'Proteins serve as enzymes, structural support like collagen and keratin, transporters like hemoglobin, and defenders like antibodies.' Cite OpenStax in the on-screen description or Path section instead.

issues.claim: Adenine pairs with thymine via two hydrogen bonds, guanine pairs with cytosine via three — that is why GC-rich DNA is more stable.
issues.problem: Accurate at AP level. A purist could note GC stability also involves base-stacking interactions, not solely H-bond count, but the College Board explicitly teaches the H-bond-count explanation, so the script is aligned with the rubric.
issues.suggested_fix: No change needed for AP rigor.

overall_assessment: Script is factually sound and AP-aligned. All chemistry (water polarity, 104.5 degree bond angle, CHNOPS, hydrogen-bond donor/acceptor definition, alpha vs beta glycosidic linkages, 3'-5' phosphodiester directionality, antiparallel strands, A-T/G-C H-bond counts, four levels of protein structure, denaturation preserving primary sequence, lipids as non-polymers built by dehydration synthesis) checks out against the AP Biology CED Unit 1. No hallucinated statistics, no fabricated researchers, no misleading mechanisms. Only stylistic flag is the in-narration OpenStax chapter citation in section 13, which is correct but reads as a pipeline artifact. Safe to keep on YouTube as unlisted; recommend one stylistic edit before any public re-release.
